title: The Derivative as a Function
objectives: Define the derivative as a function and compute derivatives using the limit definition
Calculate higher-order derivatives including second and third derivatives
Analyze the relationship between a function and its derivative graphically
Explain why differentiability implies continuity and provide counterexamples for the converse

devotional.title: Searching Out Hidden Truths
devotional.content: "It is the glory of God to conceal a matter; to search out a matter is the glory of kings." In this section, we discover that the derivative—previously computed one point at a time—is itself a function, encoding the slope at every point simultaneously. Like Isaac Newton, who saw himself as "a boy playing on the sea-shore, whilst the great ocean of truth lay all undiscovered before me," we too stand at the edge of profound mathematical truths. Each calculation reveals more of the elegant structure God has woven into creation, inviting us to search out these hidden patterns as an act of worship.

content.text: In Section 3.1 we computed $f'(a)$ for specific values of $a$: the derivative of $x^{2}$ at $a = 1$, at $a = 3$, at $a = -2$. Each computation required a fresh limit calculation. But when we collected our results—$f'(1) = 2$, $f'(3) = 6$, $f'(-2) = -4$—we noticed that every answer had the form $f'(a) = 2a$. In other words, the derivative itself is a *function*: feed it an input $x$, and it returns $f'(x) = 2x$.

This is the key insight of the present section. Instead of computing the derivative one point at a time, we leave $a$ as a variable and compute $f'(x)$ for all $x$ at once. The result is a brand-new function—one that encodes the slope of the original curve at every point.

content.title: The Derivative Function
content.content: The **derivative of $f$** is the function $f'$ defined by $$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$$ at every $x$ for which this limit exists. The domain of $f'$ is the set of all $x$ in the domain of $f$ where $f$ is differentiable.

content.text: The process of computing $f'$ is called **differentiation**. We say that we "differentiate $f$" to obtain $f'$.
Computing Derivative Functions from the Definition
We now repeat, more systematically, the computations from Section 3.1—but this time we keep the variable $x$ throughout.

content.title: Derivative of $f(x) = x^{2}$
content.problem: Find $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = x^{2}$.
content.solution: $$\begin{aligned} f'(x) &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{(x+h)^{2} - x^{2}}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{x^{2} + 2xh + h^{2} - x^{2}}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{2xh + h^{2}}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} (2x + h) \\ &= 2x. \end{aligned}$$ The derivative of $f(x) = x^{2}$ is $f'(x) = 2x$. This single formula encodes every tangent-slope calculation we did in Section 3.1: $f'(1) = 2$, $f'(3) = 6$, $f'(-2) = -4$, and so on.

content.title: Derivative of $f(x) = x^{3}$
content.problem: Find $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = x^{3}$.

content.title: Derivative of $f(x) = \frac{1}{x}$
content.problem: Find $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = \frac{1}{x}$, $x \neq 0$.
content.solution: $$\begin{aligned} f'(x) &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\frac{1}{x+h} - \frac{1}{x}}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{1}{h} \cdot \frac{x - (x+h)}{x(x+h)} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{1}{h} \cdot \frac{-h}{x(x+h)} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{-1}{x(x+h)} \\ &= \frac{-1}{x \cdot x} \\ &= -\frac{1}{x^{2}}. \end{aligned}$$ The derivative of $\frac{1}{x}$ is $-\frac{1}{x^{2}}$. Note that $f'$ is defined for all $x \neq 0$—the same domain as $f$.

content.title: Derivative of $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$
content.problem: Find $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$, $x > 0$.
content.solution: $$\begin{aligned} f'(x) &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\sqrt{x+h} - \sqrt{x}}{h} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{x+h} + \sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{x+h} + \sqrt{x}} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{(x+h) - x}{h\bigl(\sqrt{x+h} + \sqrt{x}\bigr)} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{h}{h\bigl(\sqrt{x+h} + \sqrt{x}\bigr)} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{1}{\sqrt{x+h} + \sqrt{x}} \\ &= \frac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}. \end{aligned}$$ The derivative of $\sqrt{x}$ is $\frac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}$ for $x > 0$. Observe that $f'(x) \to \infty$ as $x \to 0^{+}$, which reflects the increasingly steep rise of $\sqrt{x}$ near the origin.

content.title: Derivative of $f(x) = |x|$
content.problem: Investigate the derivative of $f(x) = |x|$.
content.solution:
We consider three cases.

**Case 1: $x > 0$.** Then for sufficiently small $|h|$, $x + h > 0$, so $|x+h| = x + h$ and $|x| = x$: $$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{(x+h) - x}{h} = \lim_{h \to 0} 1 = 1.$$

**Case 2: $x < 0$.** Then for sufficiently small $|h|$, $x + h < 0$, so $|x+h| = -(x+h)$ and $|x| = -x$: $$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{-(x+h) - (-x)}{h} = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{-h}{h} = -1.$$

**Case 3: $x = 0$.** We showed in Section 3.1 that the one-sided limits disagree: $$\lim_{h \to 0^{+}} \frac{|h|}{h} = 1, \qquad \lim_{h \to 0^{-}} \frac{|h|}{h} = -1.$$ Since $1 \neq -1$, $f'(0)$ does not exist.

Therefore: $$f'(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } x > 0, \\ -1 & \text{if } x < 0, \\ \text{undefined} & \text{if } x = 0. \end{cases}$$ This can be written as $f'(x) = \frac{x}{|x|}$ for $x \neq 0$, or equivalently $f'(x) = \operatorname{sgn}(x)$.

content.text: One of the most illuminating exercises is to graph a function and its derivative side by side. The derivative $f'(x)$ tells us the slope of $f$ at each point, so:

items: Where $f$ is **increasing** (rising from left to right), the slope is positive, so $f'(x) > 0$.
Where $f$ is **decreasing** (falling from left to right), the slope is negative, so $f'(x) < 0$.
Where $f$ has a **horizontal tangent** (a local peak or valley), the slope is zero, so $f'(x) = 0$.

content.title: Graphing $x^{2}$ and $2x$
content.problem: Sketch $f(x) = x^{2}$ and $f'(x) = 2x$ on the same axes.
content.solution:
Observe:

- For $x < 0$, $f$ is decreasing and $f'(x) = 2x < 0$.
- At $x = 0$, $f$ has its minimum and $f'(0) = 0$.
- For $x > 0$, $f$ is increasing and $f'(x) = 2x > 0$.
- As $x$ moves away from zero, $f$ gets steeper and $|f'(x)|$ grows.

The derivative function *detects* the behavior of the original function.

content.title: Graphing $x^{3}$ and $3x^{2}$
content.problem: Sketch $f(x) = x^{3}$ and $f'(x) = 3x^{2}$.
content.solution:
Key observations:

- $f(x) = x^{3}$ is always increasing (for $x \neq 0$), and $f'(x) = 3x^{2} \ge 0$ everywhere.
- At $x = 0$, $f$ has a horizontal tangent and $f'(0) = 0$, even though $f$ is not at a peak or valley. The function merely "levels off" for an instant before continuing to increase.
- $f'(x) = 3x^{2}$ is always nonnegative—it touches zero only at $x = 0$.

content.text: The derivative is so fundamental that several notations have developed over the centuries. Each has its advantages, and you will encounter all of them in mathematics and the sciences.

items: $\frac{dy}{dx}$ — read as "$dy\,dx$" or "the derivative of $y$ with respect to $x$" (Leibniz notation)
$\frac{d}{dx}[f(x)]$ — read as "$d\,dx$ of $f$ of $x$" (Leibniz operator form)
$\dot{y}$ — read as "$y$ dot" (Newton notation, used for time derivatives)

content.text: The Leibniz notation $\frac{dy}{dx}$ is particularly powerful because it reminds us what we are differentiating ($y$) and with respect to what ($x$). Although $\frac{dy}{dx}$ looks like a fraction, it is *not* literally a ratio of two numbers $dy$ and $dx$—it is a single symbol denoting the limit $$\frac{dy}{dx} = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}.$$ The "fraction-like" behavior of Leibniz notation will prove remarkably useful when we study the chain rule and integration.

content.title: Leibniz notation is not a fraction
content.content: Although $\frac{dy}{dx}$ behaves like a fraction in many formulas (a fact we will exploit later), it is defined as a *limit*. Writing $\frac{dy}{dx} = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$ is the precise meaning. Do not attempt to "cancel" $dx$ as if it were a standalone quantity—at least not yet.

content.text: When we want to evaluate the derivative at a specific point $x = a$, we write $$f'(a), \qquad \frac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{x=a}, \qquad \text{or} \qquad \left.\frac{d}{dx}[f(x)]\right|_{x=a}.$$
Since $f'$ is itself a function, we can differentiate it to obtain the **second derivative** of $f$:

content.title: Higher-Order Derivatives
content.content: The **second derivative** of $f$ is $f'' = (f')'$, the derivative of the derivative. In Leibniz notation: $$f''(x) = \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = \frac{d}{dx}\!\left[\frac{dy}{dx}\right].$$ More generally, the **$n$th derivative** of $f$ is $$f^{(n)}(x) = \frac{d^{n}y}{dx^{n}},$$ obtained by differentiating $n$ times.

content.title: Higher derivatives of $x^{3}$
content.problem: Find all derivatives of $f(x) = x^{3}$.
content.solution: We already know $f'(x) = 3x^{2}$. Now we differentiate $f'$: $$\begin{aligned} f''(x) &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{3(x+h)^{2} - 3x^{2}}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{3(x^{2} + 2xh + h^{2}) - 3x^{2}}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{6xh + 3h^{2}}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} (6x + 3h) \\ &= 6x. \end{aligned}$$ Continuing: $$\begin{aligned} f'''(x) &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{6(x+h) - 6x}{h} = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{6h}{h} = 6, \\ f^{(4)}(x) &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{6 - 6}{h} = 0. \end{aligned}$$ All subsequent derivatives are also zero: $f^{(n)}(x) = 0$ for $n \ge 4$.

content.title: Second derivative of $\sqrt{x}$
content.problem: Find $f''(x)$ for $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$.
content.solution: We know $f'(x) = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{x}} = \frac{1}{2}x^{-1/2}$. Now we differentiate $g(x) = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}$ using the limit definition: $$\begin{aligned} f''(x) &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{g(x+h) - g(x)}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\frac{1}{2\sqrt{x+h}} - \frac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}}{h} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{1}{2h} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{x} - \sqrt{x+h}}{\sqrt{x}\,\sqrt{x+h}}. \end{aligned}$$ We rationalize the numerator $\sqrt{x} - \sqrt{x+h}$ by multiplying by $\frac{\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{x+h}}{\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{x+h}}$: $$\begin{aligned} f''(x) &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{1}{2h} \cdot \frac{x - (x+h)}{\sqrt{x}\,\sqrt{x+h}\,(\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{x+h})} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{1}{2h} \cdot \frac{-h}{\sqrt{x}\,\sqrt{x+h}\,(\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{x+h})} \\ &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{-1}{2\sqrt{x}\,\sqrt{x+h}\,(\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{x+h})} \\ &= \frac{-1}{2\sqrt{x} \cdot \sqrt{x} \cdot 2\sqrt{x}} \\ &= \frac{-1}{4x\sqrt{x}} \\ &= -\frac{1}{4x^{3/2}}. \end{aligned}$$ The second derivative is negative for all $x > 0$, which means the graph of $\sqrt{x}$ is always "bending downward"—a property we call *concavity*, which we explore in Section 3.3.

content.text: We have observed that discontinuous functions are never differentiable, and that continuous functions (like $|x|$) are not always differentiable. The precise relationship is captured by the following theorem.

content.title: Differentiability Implies Continuity
content.content: If $f$ is differentiable at $a$, then $f$ is continuous at $a$.

We need to show that $\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = f(a)$. The key idea is to write $f(x) - f(a)$ in a form that involves the derivative. For $x \neq a$: $$f(x) - f(a) = \frac{f(x) - f(a)}{x - a} \cdot (x - a).$$ Taking the limit as $x \to a$: $$\begin{aligned} \lim_{x \to a} [f(x) - f(a)] &= \lim_{x \to a} \left[\frac{f(x) - f(a)}{x - a} \cdot (x - a)\right] \\ &= \left[\lim_{x \to a} \frac{f(x) - f(a)}{x - a}\right] \cdot \left[\lim_{x \to a} (x - a)\right] \\ &= f'(a) \cdot 0 \\ &= 0. \end{aligned}$$ In the second line we used the product rule for limits; both limits exist because $f'(a)$ exists (by hypothesis) and $\lim_{x \to a}(x - a) = 0$ is immediate. Since $\lim_{x \to a}[f(x) - f(a)] = 0$, we conclude that $\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = f(a)$, which is precisely the definition of continuity at $a$. ∎

content.title: The converse is false
content.content:
Continuity does *not* imply differentiability. The function $f(x) = |x|$ is continuous at $x = 0$ (you can verify that $\lim_{x \to 0} |x| = 0 = f(0)$), but as we showed in Example 3.2.5, $f'(0)$ does not exist. The corner in the graph prevents the existence of a unique tangent line.

In fact, there exist functions that are continuous *everywhere* but differentiable *nowhere*—a startling discovery due to Karl Weierstrass in 1872. Such functions are infinitely "jagged" at every scale, like a coastline that never smooths out no matter how closely you zoom in.

content.text: We can summarize the logical relationship: $$\text{differentiable at } a \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{continuous at } a,$$ but the reverse implication $\Longleftarrow$ is **false**.

content.title: Derivative of $f(x) = \frac{1}{x+1}$
content.problem: Find $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = \frac{1}{x+1}$, $x \neq -1$.

content.title: Derivative of $f(x) = x^{2} + 3x$
content.problem: Find $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = x^{2} + 3x$.
Use the limit definition to find $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = 3x - 7$.
Use the limit definition to find $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = x^{2} + 1$.
Use the limit definition to find $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = 2x^{2} - x$.
Use the limit definition to find $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = x^{2} + 5x - 3$.
Use the limit definition to find $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = x^{3} - x$.
Use the limit definition to find $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{2x}$, $x \neq 0$.
Use the limit definition to find $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x-1}$, $x \neq 1$.
Use the limit definition to find $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = \sqrt{x + 1}$, $x > -1$.
Use the limit definition to find $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = \sqrt{3x}$, $x > 0$.
Use the limit definition to find $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = 4x^{2} + 2x + 1$.
Given that $f(x) = x^{2} - 4x$, compute $f'(x)$ from the definition. Then sketch $f$ and $f'$ on the same axes. At what value of $x$ does $f$ have a horizontal tangent?
Sketch $f(x) = |x|$ and its derivative $f'(x)$ on the same axes. Explain in your own words why the derivative graph has a gap at $x = 0$.
The graph of a function $f$ is shown as a smooth curve that rises, levels off, then falls. Sketch the graph of $f'$.
A function $f$ is increasing on $(-\infty, 1)$ and decreasing on $(1, \infty)$. The curve is smooth everywhere. Sketch a possible graph of $f'$. What can you say about $f'(1)$?
Find $f'(x)$ and $f''(x)$ for $f(x) = x^{2} + 3x$. Use the limit definition for both.
Find $f''(x)$ for $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$ using the limit definition applied to $f'(x) = -\dfrac{1}{x^{2}}$.
If $f(x) = x^{4}$, find $f'(x)$ from the limit definition. *Hint:* $(x+h)^{4} = x^{4} + 4x^{3}h + 6x^{2}h^{2} + 4xh^{3} + h^{4}$.
Determine whether $f(x) = |x - 2|$ is differentiable at $x = 2$. Justify using the limit definition.
Determine whether $f(x) = x^{1/3}$ is differentiable at $x = 0$. Compute the relevant limit and explain.
Let $f(x) = \begin{cases} x^{2} & x \le 1 \\ 2x - 1 & x > 1.\end{cases}$ Is $f$ differentiable at $x = 1$? Check continuity first, then compute the one-sided limits of the difference quotient.
Use the limit definition to find $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = \dfrac{x}{x+1}$, $x \neq -1$.
Use the limit definition to find $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = \sqrt{x^{2}+1}$. *Hint:* Rationalize the numerator.
Let $f(x) = x|x|$. Show that $f$ is differentiable at $x = 0$ and find $f'(0)$. Is $f'$ continuous at $0$? Is $f''(0)$ defined?
For what value(s) of $a$ and $b$ is the function $f(x) = \begin{cases} x^{2} & x \le 1 \\ ax + b & x > 1 \end{cases}$ differentiable at $x = 1$?
Prove that if $f$ is differentiable at $a$ and $c$ is a constant, then $g(x) = c \cdot f(x)$ is differentiable at $a$ and $g'(a) = c \cdot f'(a)$.
Prove that if $f$ and $g$ are both differentiable at $a$, then $s(x) = f(x) + g(x)$ is differentiable at $a$ and $s'(a) = f'(a) + g'(a)$.

Suppose $f$ is differentiable at $a$ and $f'(a) \neq 0$. Show that $f$ is one-to-one on some interval containing $a$. *Hint:* Use the fact that the sign of the difference quotient is eventually constant near $a$.

The height of a sunflower (in cm) after $t$ weeks of growth is modeled by $h(t) = 200 - \dfrac{180}{t+1}$ for $t \ge 0$. Find $h'(t)$ from the limit definition. Then compute $h'(2)$ and $h'(8)$. During which period is the sunflower growing faster?

The concentration of a drug in the bloodstream $t$ hours after injection is modeled by $C(t) = \dfrac{10}{t+1}$ mg/L. Find $C'(t)$ using the limit definition. Is the concentration increasing or decreasing? At what rate is it changing at $t = 4$ hours?
